entries.name: Baraita
entries.notes: Tannaitic teaching not included in the Mishnah; cited in the Gemara as supporting or challenging evidence

entries.name: Teiku / Taku (Unresolved Question)
entries.notes: Designation for a Talmudic question left unresolved; various interpretations of the term exist

entries.name: Ibaya Lehu (The Question Was Raised)
entries.notes: Standard formula introducing an unresolved query in the Gemara

entries.name: Asmachta (Exegetical Support)
entries.notes: A Scriptural peg used to support a rabbinic ruling without being its actual source

entries.name: Deorayta vs. Derabanan
entries.notes: Distinction between Torah-level obligations and rabbinic enactments; critical for doubt-resolution and leniency

entries.name: Takkanah (Rabbinic Enactment)
entries.notes: Positive rabbinic legislation instituting new practice

entries.name: Gezeirah (Rabbinic Protective Fence)
entries.notes: Preventive rabbinic prohibition enacted as a 'fence' around Torah law

entries.name: Gezeirah l'Gezeirah (Double Rabbinic Fence)
entries.notes: Rabbinic fence upon a rabbinic fence; generally not permitted

entries.name: Minhag (Custom)
entries.notes: Established communal or individual custom; legal weight and relationship to formal law

entries.name: Minhag haMedinah (Regional Custom)
entries.notes: Local or regional custom that carries legal weight in commercial and labor law

entries.name: Hora'at Sha'ah (Temporary Ruling)
entries.notes: One-time emergency ruling that does not set permanent precedent

entries.name: Halachah leMoshe miSinai
entries.notes: Laws transmitted orally from Sinai without explicit Scriptural derivation

entries.name: Dibrah Torah k'Lshon Bnei Adam
entries.notes: Principle that the Torah speaks in ordinary human language; affects how repetitions and style are interpreted

entries.name: Lo Plug (No Distinction Made)
entries.notes: Principle that a rabbinic ruling applies uniformly without exception, even where the reason does not apply

entries.name: Davar sheb'Minyan
entries.notes: A rabbinic ruling enacted by formal count that requires another formal vote to overturn

entries.name: Toch Kedei Dibbur
entries.notes: The brief moment following a statement during which a retraction or addition is treated as simultaneous

entries.name: Kofin (Court Compulsion)
entries.notes: Cases where the court compels compliance with a positive obligation

entries.name: Lifnim miShurat haDin (Beyond the Letter of the Law)
entries.notes: Acting beyond strict legal requirement; sometimes judicially enforced

entries.name: Dina d'Malchuta Dina (The Law of the Land is Law)
entries.notes: Principle recognizing secular governmental law as binding in civil matters

entries.name: Mutav sheyihyu Shogigin
entries.notes: Better that people sin inadvertently than deliberately; rationale for not publicizing certain prohibitions

entries.name: Oness Rachmana Patreih (The Merciful One Exempts the Compelled)
entries.notes: Principle that one acting under duress is exempt from liability

entries.name: Guzma (Talmudic Exaggeration)
entries.notes: Hyperbolic or rhetorical overstatement in rabbinic literature not meant literally

entries.name: Talmudic Lists
entries.notes: Structural enumerations (e.g., 'These are the four...') as a pedagogic and mnemonic device

entries.name: Milta d'Lo Schechicha Lo Gazru Bei Rabbanan
entries.hebrew: מִילְּתָא דְּלָא שְׁכִיחָא לָא גָּזְרוּ בֵּיהּ רַבָּנָן
entries.notes: Rabbis did not legislate for uncommon situations

entries.name: Ein Ruach Chachamim Nochah Heimenu
entries.notes: The Sages are displeased with a certain behavior; non-binding disapproval short of formal prohibition

entries.name: Koach d'Hetera Adif (The Power of Leniency is Greater)
entries.notes: Where possible, a decisor should justify leniency rather than stringency

entries.name: Lo Mibaya (It Goes Without Saying)
entries.notes: Rhetorical formula indicating the obvious case, leading to the novel one

entries.name: Lehodia Kochan (To Publicize Their Power)
entries.notes: A ruling is stated to demonstrate the full scope of the Sages' authority

entries.name: Batlah Da'ato Eitzel Kol Adam
entries.notes: One's opinion is nullified in relation to all others; standard for minority nullification

entries.name: Gematria (Numerical Value Exegesis)
entries.notes: Interpretive method using numerical value of Hebrew letters

entries.name: Shibuda d'Rabbi Natan
entries.notes: Creditor of a debtor has a claim against the debtor's creditor; principle of indirect debt collection

entries.name: Dichuy (Displacement / Rejection)
entries.notes: Once an item or person is displaced from eligibility, whether it can be reinstated

entries.name: Ein Osin Mitzvot Chavilot Chavilot
entries.notes: One should not perform mitzvot in bundles; each deserves individual attention

entries.name: Ein Omrin l'Adam Chatay Bishvil sheyizkeh
entries.hebrew: אֵין אוֹמְרִין לְאָדָם חֲטָא בִּשְׁבִיל שֶׁיִּזְכֶּה
entries.notes: One may not sin in order to benefit another person

entries.name: Mitzvah min haMuvchar (Preferred Manner of Mitzvah Performance)
entries.notes: The optimal or most beautiful way to fulfill a commandment

entries.name: Mitzvah haBa'ah ba'Aveirah
entries.notes: A mitzvah performed by means of a transgression is invalid or disqualified

entries.name: Zerizin Makdimin l'Mitzvot
entries.notes: The diligent perform mitzvot at the earliest opportunity

entries.name: Ein Ma'avirin al haMitzvot
entries.notes: One should not pass over a mitzvah opportunity to perform a later one

entries.name: Ha'oseh b'Mitzvah Patur
entries.notes: One engaged in one mitzvah is exempt from performing another simultaneously

entries.name: Mitzvot Aseh shehaZ'man Gerama
entries.notes: Time-bound positive commandments from which women are exempt

entries.name: Hermeneutical Principles
entries.notes: The formal rules (middot) by which the Torah is interpreted; primarily the 13 Middot of Rabbi Yishmael

entries.name: Kal vaChomer (A Fortiori Inference)
entries.notes: If X is true in a lenient case, it is certainly true in a stricter case. First of 13 Middot.

entries.name: Gezerah Shavah (Verbal Analogy)
entries.notes: Inference drawn from identical or analogous words appearing in two different passages. Second of 13 Middot.

entries.name: Binyan Av (Building a General Principle)
entries.notes: A principle derived from one verse or two verses applied to other cases. Third of 13 Middot.

entries.name: Kelal uFerat (General and Particular)
entries.notes: When a general statement is followed by a particular example, the law is limited to the particular. Fourth of 13 Middot.

entries.name: Perat uKlal (Particular and General)
entries.notes: When a particular precedes a general, the general expands the particular. Fifth of 13 Middot.

entries.name: Kelal uFerat uKlal
entries.notes: General-particular-general: law applies to cases similar to the particular. Sixth of 13 Middot.

entries.name: Kelal shehu Tzarich liPerat
entries.notes: A general statement requiring a particular to explain it. Seventh of 13 Middot.

entries.name: Perat shehu Tzarich liKlal
entries.notes: A particular requiring its general to explain it. Eighth of 13 Middot.

entries.name: Exceptions from General Rules (Middot 9-11)
entries.notes: Three variants dealing with cases that deviate from an established general rule. Middot 9-11 of Rabbi Yishmael.

entries.name: Davar haLamed meInyano (Inference from Context)
entries.notes: Meaning derived from the surrounding context of a passage. Twelfth of 13 Middot.

entries.name: Shnei Ketuvim haMachishim (Two Contradictory Verses)
entries.notes: Two contradictory verses resolved by a third. Thirteenth of 13 Middot.

entries.name: Hekesh (Scriptural Juxtaposition)
entries.notes: Analogy drawn from two subjects placed adjacent in a Biblical passage

entries.name: Lav haBa miChlal Aseh
entries.notes: A negative prohibition derived by implication from a positive commandment

entries.name: Lav shebikhlalot (Prohibition Subsumed in a General Rule)
entries.notes: A prohibition that is part of a general category; consequences for lashes

entries.name: Lav haNitak la'Aseh
entries.notes: A prohibition rectifiable by a subsequent positive act; affects lashes and atonement

entries.name: Lav haNitan l'Azharat Mitat Beit Din
entries.hebrew: לָאו הַנִּיתַּן לְאַזְהָרַת מִיתַת בֵּית דִּין
entries.notes: A prohibition serving as the warning for a capital offense; not punishable by lashes

entries.name: Aseh Docheh Lo Taaseh
entries.notes: A positive commandment overrides a negative prohibition when they conflict

entries.name: Ein Issur Chal al Issur
entries.notes: A prohibition cannot take effect on something already prohibited; exceptions include issur kolel and issur mosif

entries.name: Issur Kolel (Encompassing Prohibition)
entries.notes: A new prohibition that adds additional items to a pre-existing prohibition can take effect

entries.name: Issur Mosif (Adding Prohibition)
entries.notes: A new prohibition that adds stringency to a pre-existing one can take effect

entries.name: Lavud (Constructive Connection)
entries.notes: Items within three tefachim of each other are considered connected; used in laws of sukkah and partitions

entries.name: Safek (Doubt)
entries.notes: Halakhic uncertainty and rules for resolution; stricter at Torah level than rabbinic level

entries.name: Sfek Sfeika (Double Doubt)
entries.notes: Two layers of doubt; permits leniency even at Torah level under certain conditions

entries.name: Rov (Majority)
entries.notes: Follow the majority; one of the primary mechanisms for resolving doubt

entries.name: Kol d'Parish meRuba Parish
entries.notes: Anything that separates from a group is assumed to have come from the majority

entries.name: Kol Kavua k'Mechetzah al Mechetzah Dami
entries.hebrew: כָּל קָבוּעַ כְּמֶחֱצָה עַל מֶחֱצָה דָּמֵי
entries.notes: When the item is in a fixed location, majority does not apply; treated as 50-50

entries.name: Chazakah (Presumptive Status)
entries.notes: Legal presumption that a prior status continues; multiple types including chezkat hamatzui and chezkat haguf

entries.name: Umdena (Logical Assumption)
entries.notes: Reasonable assessment of intent or situation used to adjudicate

entries.name: Umdena deMukhach (Compelling Assumption)
entries.notes: A clear and convincing inference about intent that can override explicit statements

entries.name: Breira (Retroactive Clarification)
entries.notes: Whether a later event can retroactively determine the status of an earlier situation

entries.name: Kavua (Fixed Status)
entries.notes: When an item remains in its fixed location, different rules of doubt apply than when it separates

entries.name: Miggo (Since He Could Have)
entries.notes: A claim is believed because the claimant could have made a more advantageous claim

entries.name: Ho'il (Since / Because)
entries.notes: Logical extension: since a possibility exists, the object is treated as if it were already that thing

entries.name: Psik Reisha (Inevitable Consequence)
entries.notes: An inevitable prohibited result of an action renders the action intentional, even if unintended

entries.name: Davar she'Eino Mitkavein
entries.notes: An unintended act; generally permitted unless its prohibited consequence is inevitable (psik reisha)

entries.name: Melacha she'Einah Tzerichah l'Gufah
entries.notes: A Shabbat labor performed for a purpose other than its standard outcome; Rabbi Shimon exempts

entries.name: Mitasek (Inadvertent Unaware Action)
entries.notes: One who performs a prohibited act without awareness of doing so; generally exempt

entries.name: Grama (Indirect Causation)
entries.notes: Indirect causation of damage; generally exempt from monetary liability but morally prohibited

entries.name: Lifnei Iver (Placing a Stumbling Block)
entries.notes: Prohibition against causing another to sin or giving harmful advice

entries.name: Ein Shaliach l'Dvar Aveirah
entries.notes: An agent who commits a sin is personally liable; the sender cannot transfer responsibility via agency

entries.name: Kol haRa'uy l'Beeilah (Applies Only to What is Eligible)
entries.notes: Only that which is fit for mixing requires mixing; used in minchah and other offering contexts

entries.name: Min b'Mino and Min b'she'Eino Mino
entries.notes: Like-kind and unlike-kind mixtures; different nullification rules apply

entries.name: Circumstantial Evidence
entries.notes: Use of indirect evidence in Talmudic legal proceedings

entries.name: Rabbinic Institutions and Courts
entries.notes: Formal bodies of rabbinic governance, adjudication, and teaching

entries.name: Sanhedrin Gedolah (Great Sanhedrin of 71)
entries.notes: Supreme court of 71 judges in Jerusalem; jurisdiction over capital cases, the king, and national issues

entries.name: Sanhedrin Ketanah (Small Sanhedrin of 23)
entries.notes: Regional court of 23 judges with capital jurisdiction

entries.name: Beit Din of Three
entries.notes: Minimal court for monetary cases (dinei mamonot)

entries.name: Nasi (Patriarch)
entries.notes: Head of the Sanhedrin and leader of the Jewish community in Roman Palestine

entries.name: Av Beit Din
entries.notes: President of the court; second-highest position in the Sanhedrin

entries.name: Babylonian Yeshivot (Academies)
entries.notes: The great academies of Sura and Pumbedita that shaped the Babylonian Talmud

entries.name: Academy of Sura
entries.notes: Founded by Rav; major center of Babylonian Torah learning

entries.name: Academy of Pumbedita
entries.notes: Founded by Rav Yehudah bar Yechezkel; rival academy to Sura

entries.name: Academy of Yavneh
entries.notes: Re-established by Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai after destruction of the Temple; seat of the reconstituted Sanhedrin

entries.name: Beit Midrash (House of Study)
entries.notes: House of learning; institution for Torah study and rabbinic discourse

entries.name: Reish Galuta (Exilarch)
entries.notes: Political head of the Jewish community in Babylonia; relationship with the yeshivot

entries.name: Hora'ah (Authoritative Ruling)
entries.notes: The authority to issue practical halakhic decisions; not granted to every sage

entries.name: Semichah (Rabbinic Ordination)
entries.notes: Formal ordination transmitting judicial authority; ceased in Palestine in the 4th century

entries.name: Tannaim
entries.notes: Rabbinic sages of the Mishnaic period (c. 10–220 CE) whose opinions are recorded in the Mishnah and Baraita

entries.name: Hillel
entries.notes: Head of the school of Beit Hillel; known for lenient rulings and ethical teachings; fl. late 1st c. BCE

entries.name: Shammai
entries.notes: Head of the school of Beit Shammai; typically stricter than Hillel; fl. late 1st c. BCE

entries.name: Beit Hillel (School of Hillel)
entries.notes: The halakhic school following Hillel's approach; generally lenient; halakhah follows Beit Hillel

entries.name: Beit Shammai (School of Shammai)
entries.notes: The halakhic school following Shammai's approach; generally strict; frequently cited for its stringent positions

entries.name: Rabban Gamliel
entries.notes: Nasi at Yavneh; standardized prayer and unified halakhah post-Destruction; fl. c. 80–110 CE

entries.name: Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai
entries.notes: Founded the academy at Yavneh; enacted takanot after the Temple's destruction; fl. c. 30–90 CE

entries.name: Rabbi Eliezer ben Hyrcanus
entries.notes: Student of Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai; 'lined cistern that loses not a drop'; often strict; excommunicated in Achnai debate

entries.name: Rabbi Yehoshua
entries.notes: Colleague of R. Eliezer; prominent debater; fl. c. 70–130 CE

entries.name: Rabbi Akiva
entries.notes: One of the most prominent Tannaim; systematized halakhah; teacher of Rabbi Meir; martyred by Romans

entries.name: Rabbi Yishmael
entries.notes: Formulated the 13 Middot of interpretation; often in dispute with Rabbi Akiva on exegetical method

entries.name: Rabbi Tarfon
entries.notes: Contemporary of Rabbi Akiva; priestly background; known for his emphatic and sometimes exacting rulings

entries.name: Rabbi Meir
entries.notes: Chief student of Rabbi Akiva; Stam Mishnah attributed to him; his opinions are very sharp

entries.name: Rabbi Yehudah bar Ilai
entries.notes: Most frequently cited Tanna in the Mishnah; student of Rabbi Akiva; halakhah often follows him

entries.name: Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai
entries.notes: Student of Rabbi Akiva; mystical dimension; authored Zohar according to tradition; hid in cave from Romans

entries.name: Rabbi Yosi bar Chalafta
entries.notes: Student of Rabbi Akiva; known for precision; halakhah frequently follows his view

entries.name: Rabbi Elazar ben Azaryah
entries.notes: Appointed Nasi temporarily; descended from Ezra; noted for his wisdom and aristocratic background

entries.name: Ben Azzai
entries.notes: Prominent Tanna; student who never married; one of the four who entered the Pardes

entries.name: Ben Zoma
entries.notes: Renowned for his wisdom and aggadic insights; one of the four who entered the Pardes

entries.name: Rabbi Yehudah haNasi (Rebbi)
entries.notes: Redactor of the Mishnah; Nasi of the Sanhedrin; known simply as 'Rebbi'; close to Roman authorities

entries.name: Bar Kappara
entries.notes: Student of Rabbi Yehudah haNasi; transitional figure between Tannaitic and Amoraic periods

entries.name: Amoraim
entries.notes: Rabbinic sages of the Talmudic period (c. 220–500 CE) whose discussions form the Gemara

entries.name: Palestinian Amoraim
entries.notes: Amoraim active in Eretz Yisrael (c. 220–375 CE); discussions preserved primarily in the Jerusalem Talmud

entries.name: Babylonian Amoraim
entries.notes: Amoraim active in Babylonia (c. 220–500 CE); discussions form the Babylonian Talmud

entries.name: Rabbi Yochanan bar Nafcha
entries.notes: Primary redactor of the Jerusalem Talmud; principal authority of the first generation of Palestinian Amoraim

entries.name: Reish Lakish (Rabbi Shimon ben Lakish)
entries.notes: Constant disputant with Rabbi Yochanan; brother-in-law; known for sharp argumentation

entries.name: Rabbi Chanina
entries.notes: Senior Palestinian Amora; known for his piety and healing ability

entries.name: Rabbi Oshiya
entries.notes: Early Palestinian Amora; known for his encyclopedic knowledge of Baraitot

entries.name: Rabbi Yannai
entries.notes: Early Palestinian Amora; teacher of Rabbi Yochanan

entries.name: Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi
entries.notes: First-generation Palestinian Amora; known for his aggadic teachings and legendary stories

entries.name: Rabbi Elazar ben Pedat
entries.notes: Second-generation Palestinian Amora; prominent student of Rabbi Yochanan; transmits many rulings

entries.name: Rabbi Ami
entries.notes: Third-generation Palestinian Amora; student of Rabbi Yochanan; leader of the Tiberias academy

entries.name: Rabbi Asi
entries.notes: Third-generation Palestinian Amora; colleague of Rabbi Ami; often cited together with him

entries.name: Rabbi Abbahu
entries.notes: Third-generation Palestinian Amora based in Caesarea; engaged with Roman-Christian polemics

entries.name: Rav Zeira
entries.notes: Babylonian-born Amora who emigrated to Palestine; known for extreme piety and asceticism

entries.name: Rabbi Jeremiah
entries.notes: Fourth-generation Palestinian Amora; famous for his sharp hypothetical edge cases

entries.name: Rabbi Mana
entries.notes: Fifth-generation Palestinian Amora; one of the last major sages of the Jerusalem Talmud

entries.name: Rav (Abba Areka)
entries.notes: Founder of the Sura academy; first major Babylonian Amora; student of Rabbi Yehudah haNasi; fl. c. 175–247 CE

entries.name: Shmuel
entries.notes: Head of Nehardea academy; colleague of Rav; expert in civil law and astronomy; formulated Dina d'Malchuta Dina

entries.name: Rav Huna
entries.notes: Second-generation Babylonian Amora; head of Sura after Rav; led 800 students; known for charity

entries.name: Rav Yehudah bar Yechezkel
entries.notes: Founded Pumbedita academy; student of Rav and Shmuel; noted for precise transmission of teachings

entries.name: Rav Chisda
entries.notes: Second/third generation Babylonian Amora; head of Sura; known for his sharp questions

entries.name: Rav Sheshet
entries.notes: Second/third generation Babylonian Amora; blind but possessed extraordinary memory of Baraitot

entries.name: Rav Nachman bar Yaakov
entries.notes: Second/third generation Babylonian Amora; expert in civil law; son-in-law of the Exilarch

entries.name: Rabba bar Nachmani
entries.notes: Third-generation Babylonian Amora; head of Pumbedita; known as 'Uprooter of Mountains' for sharp analysis

entries.name: Rav Yosef
entries.notes: Third-generation Babylonian Amora; head of Pumbedita; 'Sinai' for his encyclopedic knowledge; lost memory and recovered it

entries.name: Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak
entries.notes: Third/fourth generation Babylonian Amora; succeeded Rava as head of Pumbedita

entries.name: Abaye
entries.notes: Fourth-generation Babylonian Amora; head of Pumbedita; perpetual disputant with Rava; halakhah follows Rava except in 6 cases (YaL KGaM)

entries.name: Rava
entries.notes: Fourth-generation Babylonian Amora; head of Machoza; the dominant halakhic voice in most Talmudic disputes with Abaye

entries.name: Rav Papa
entries.notes: Fifth-generation Babylonian Amora; student of Abaye and Rava; head of Naresh; known for mediating positions

entries.name: Rav Ashi
entries.notes: Fifth/sixth generation; head of Sura for 60 years; principal redactor of the Babylonian Talmud

entries.name: Ravina
entries.notes: Co-redactor of the Babylonian Talmud with Rav Ashi; 'Rav Ashi and Ravina — end of instruction'

entries.name: Geonim and Rishonim
entries.notes: Post-Talmudic halakhic authorities: Geonim (600–1050) and Rishonim (1050–1500)

entries.name: Geonim (600–1050 CE)
entries.notes: Heads of the Babylonian academies of Sura and Pumbedita who served as supreme halakhic authorities

entries.name: Rav Saadia Gaon
entries.notes: 882–942 CE; Head of Sura; philosopher, polemicist, and halakhist; authored Emunot v'Deot

entries.name: Rav Sherira Gaon
entries.notes: c. 906–1006 CE; Head of Pumbedita; famous epistle (Iggeret Rav Sherira Gaon) on Talmudic history

entries.name: Rav Hai Gaon
entries.notes: 939–1038 CE; Last great Gaon; son of Sherira; prolific responsa author; head of Pumbedita

entries.name: Rav Amram Gaon
entries.notes: d. 875 CE; Head of Sura; compiled the first comprehensive Siddur (Seder Rav Amram Gaon)

entries.name: Rishonim (1050–1500 CE)
entries.notes: Medieval halakhic authorities whose commentaries and codes form the bedrock of later halakhic decisions

entries.name: Rashi (Rabbi Shlomo Yitzchaki)
entries.notes: France, 1040–1105; Wrote the primary commentary on the Talmud and Torah; essential for Talmud study

entries.name: Rabbenu Tam (Rabbi Yaakov ben Meir)
entries.notes: France, 1100–1171; Grandson of Rashi; leader of the Tosafist school; Sefer haYashar

entries.name: Ri (Rabbi Yitzchak ben Shmuel of Dampierre)
entries.notes: France, 1120–1200; Successor to Rabbenu Tam as leader of the Tosafists

entries.name: Tosafot
entries.notes: School of French and German medieval commentators (c. 1100–1300); dialectical critique of Rashi printed in standard Talmud editions

entries.name: Rif (Rabbi Yitzchak Alfasi)
entries.notes: Morocco/Spain, 1013–1103; Wrote Sefer haHalachot, the first major halakhic digest of the Talmud

entries.name: Rambam (Maimonides, Rabbi Moshe ben Maimon)
entries.notes: Spain/Egypt, 1138–1204; Wrote Mishneh Torah (code) and Moreh Nevuchim; systematic philosopher and posek

entries.name: Ramban (Nachmanides, Rabbi Moshe ben Nachman)
entries.notes: Spain/Israel, 1194–1270; Major commentator on Torah and Talmud; defended Judaism in Barcelona Disputation

entries.name: Rashba (Rabbi Shlomo ben Aderet)
entries.notes: Spain, 1235–1310; Student of Ramban; wrote novellae on most of the Talmud and thousands of responsa

entries.name: Rosh (Rabbi Asher ben Yechiel)
entries.notes: Germany/Spain, 1259–1327; Wrote Piskei haRosh; combined Tosafist methodology with Sephardic tradition

entries.name: Tur (Rabbi Yaakov ben Asher, Ba'al haTurim)
entries.notes: Spain, 1269–1343; Son of the Rosh; wrote Arba'ah Turim, the four-part code that preceded the Shulchan Aruch

entries.name: Ritva (Rabbi Yom Tov ben Avraham Alashvili)
entries.notes: Spain, 1250–1330; Wrote novellae on the Talmud; student of Rashba and Ra'ah

entries.name: Meiri (Rabbi Menachem haMeiri)
entries.notes: France, 1249–1310; Wrote Beit haBechira, a comprehensive Talmud commentary; liberal views on non-Jews

entries.name: Ran (Rabbi Nissim of Gerona)
entries.notes: Spain, 1320–1376; Wrote novellae on the Talmud and commentary on the Rif

entries.name: Mordechai (Rabbi Mordechai ben Hillel)
entries.notes: Germany, 1250–1298; Compiled Sefer Mordechai, an important halakhic digest; martyred in the Rindfleisch massacres

entries.name: Sefer haChinuch
entries.notes: Spain, 13th c.; Anonymous work (attributed to Aaron haLevi of Barcelona) enumerating the 613 commandments by parashah

entries.name: Acharonim
entries.notes: Post-Rishonim halakhic authorities (1500–present) who comment on the Shulchan Aruch and continue the halakhic tradition

entries.name: Beit Yosef / Shulchan Aruch (Rabbi Yosef Karo)
entries.notes: Spain/Tzfat, 1488–1575; Wrote the Beit Yosef commentary on the Tur and the Shulchan Aruch code; principal Sephardic authority

entries.name: Rama (Rabbi Moshe Isserles)
entries.notes: Poland, 1530–1572; Added glosses (HaMapah) to the Shulchan Aruch representing Ashkenazic custom; principal Ashkenazic authority

entries.name: Maharsha (Rabbi Shmuel Eidels)
entries.notes: Poland, 1555–1631; Wrote Chiddushei Halachot and Chiddushei Aggadot on the Talmud; printed in standard editions

entries.name: Maharshal (Rabbi Shlomo Luria)
entries.notes: Poland, 1510–1573; Wrote Yam shel Shlomo and Chochmat Shlomo; independent critical approach to Talmudic text

entries.name: Magen Avraham (Rabbi Avraham Gombiner)
entries.notes: Poland, 1633–1683; Major commentary on Orach Chaim section of Shulchan Aruch

entries.name: Sha'agat Aryeh (Rabbi Aryeh Leib Gunzberg)
entries.notes: Lithuania, 1695–1785; Rigorous halakhic responsa; known for sharp analytical style

entries.name: Vilna Gaon (Rabbi Eliyahu of Vilna, GRA)
entries.notes: Lithuania, 1720–1797; Master of all areas of Torah; wrote Biur haGra on Shulchan Aruch; opponent of Hasidism

entries.name: Chatam Sofer (Rabbi Moshe Sofer)
entries.notes: Hungary, 1762–1839; Major opponent of religious reform; thousands of responsa; 'Chadash asur min haTorah'

entries.name: Aruch HaShulchan (Rabbi Yechiel Michel Epstein)
entries.notes: Russia, 1829–1908; Comprehensive code following the structure of the Shulchan Aruch; integrates minhag and halakhah

entries.name: Mishnah Berurah (Chofetz Chaim, Rabbi Yisrael Meir Kagan)
entries.notes: Poland, 1838–1933; Authoritative commentary on Orach Chaim; standard modern Ashkenazic halakhic reference

entries.name: Chazon Ish (Rabbi Avraham Yeshaya Karelitz)
entries.notes: Israel, 1878–1953; Wrote comprehensive works on all areas of halakhah; major influence on Israeli haredi community

entries.name: Israel vs. Bavel (Eretz Yisrael and Babylonian Traditions)
entries.notes: Differences in halakhic tradition, calendar, and practice between the two major centers

entries.name: Ibbur Shanah (Intercalation of the Year)
entries.notes: The process of declaring a leap year; jurisdiction of the Sanhedrin or authorized court

entries.name: Safek d'Orayta and Safek d'Rabbanan
entries.notes: Distinction between doubt in Torah-level and rabbinic-level law; stricter treatment of Torah-level doubt

entries.name: Asmachta (Conditional Transactions)
entries.notes: An overly optimistic conditional transaction where full intent to transfer ownership may be lacking; generally not enforceable

entries.name: Urim veTummim
entries.notes: Divine oracular device on the High Priest's breastplate; used for national decisions; related to resolving doubtful cases

entries.name: Rabbinic Edicts and Enactments
entries.notes: General category of post-biblical legislation; includes both takkanot (positive) and gezeirot (preventive)

entries.name: Rov and Miut (Majority and Minority)
entries.notes: Halakhic rule that the majority determines the norm; minority status and its treatment

entries.name: The Sages (Chachamim)
entries.notes: The anonymous rabbinic majority opinion, often cited as 'Chachamim say' in contrast to individual named opinions

entries.name: Miluim (Contextual/Supplementary Analysis)
entries.notes: Supplementary exegetical analysis adding to or completing a Talmudic discussion

entries.name: Tannaim (General Category in Methodology)
entries.notes: Status and authority of Tannaitic sources in Amoraic argumentation; 'Tanna contradicts Tanna'

entries.name: Amoraim (General Category in Methodology)
entries.notes: Status of Amoraic authority relative to Tannaitic sources; an Amora cannot dispute a Tanna without support

entries.name: 13 Middot of Rabbi Yishmael
entries.notes: The canonical thirteen hermeneutical principles recited in the morning liturgy; basis for legal midrash

entries.name: Uvda d'Chol beShabbat v'Yom Tov
entries.notes: Weekday activities performed on Shabbat or Yom Tov that violate the spirit of the day even if technically permitted

entries.name: Peri Megadim (Rabbi Yosef ben Meir Teomim)
entries.notes: Germany/Poland, 1727–1792; Supercommentary on the Magen Avraham and Turei Zahav; standard reference

entries.name: Taz — Turei Zahav (Rabbi David HaLevi Segal)
entries.notes: Poland, 1586–1667; Major commentary on the Shulchan Aruch, particularly Yoreh Deah and Orach Chaim

entries.name: Shach — Siftei Kohen (Rabbi Shabbetai haKohen)
entries.notes: Lithuania, 1621–1662; Major commentary on Yoreh Deah and Choshen Mishpat of Shulchan Aruch

entries.name: Noda biYehudah (Rabbi Yechezkel Landau)
entries.notes: Prague, 1713–1793; Major responsa collection; authoritative voice in 18th century halakhah

entries.name: Beit Shmuel (Rabbi Shmuel ben Uri Shraga Phoebus)
entries.notes: Poland, 17th c.; Commentary on Even haEzer section of Shulchan Aruch; standard reference on family law

entries.name: Chida (Rabbi Chaim Yosef David Azulai)
entries.notes: Israel/Italy, 1724–1806; Prolific author and bibliographer; Shem haGedolim and Birkei Yosef

entries.name: Takkanot of Usha
entries.notes: Enactments made at Usha after the Bar Kokhba revolt reconstituting rabbinic leadership in the Galilee

entries.name: Takkanot of Ezra
entries.notes: Enactments attributed to Ezra the Scribe establishing communal and personal religious standards

entries.name: Anshei Knesset haGedolah (Men of the Great Assembly)
entries.notes: Post-prophetic body of 120 elders that canonized Scripture, instituted prayer, and established many practices

entries.name: Tenai Kaful (Double Condition)
entries.notes: A valid legal condition must be stated in both positive and negative form (learned from the condition of the tribes of Gad and Reuven)

entries.name: Kol haRa'uy liBeilah — Eligibility Requirement
entries.notes: Only items fit for the required process are subject to that process's requirement

entries.name: Peshara (Compromise)
entries.notes: Court-mediated settlement as an alternative to strict legal judgment

entries.name: Zaken Mamre (Rebellious Elder)
entries.notes: A scholar who defies the Great Sanhedrin's ruling after deliberation; capital offense

entries.name: Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel
entries.notes: Nasi; frequently cited in the Mishnah; his rulings are generally followed; father of Rabbi Yehudah haNasi

entries.name: Talmud Lomar (Scripture Teaches)
entries.notes: Standard formula introducing a Scriptural derivation of a halakhic point

entries.name: Mah haTzad (What is the Common Denominator?)
entries.notes: Argument by analogy based on a shared characteristic; type of logical inference in Talmudic reasoning

entries.name: Pircha (Logical Objection)
entries.notes: A refutation of an analogy by pointing out a distinguishing characteristic

entries.name: Tzrichuta (Necessity of Repetition)
entries.notes: Explanation of why both of two seemingly redundant teachings are necessary

entries.name: Sevara (Legal Reasoning / Common Sense)
entries.notes: Logical reasoning that serves as an independent halakhic source; 'sevara is like the Torah itself'

entries.name: Chiddush (Novel Legal Ruling)
entries.notes: A novel legal ruling that cannot be derived by logic alone; once stated, is not extended further

entries.name: Ikka d'Amrei / Itimar (Alternative Tradition)
entries.notes: Formula introducing an alternative version of a previously cited ruling or discussion

entries.name: Meikha / Itibei (Objection from Mishnah or Baraita)
entries.notes: Standard formula for raising a contradiction from a Tannaitic source against an Amoraic ruling

entries.name: Tartei d'Satrei (Internal Contradiction)
entries.notes: Two contradictory elements within a single ruling or source; requires resolution

entries.name: Kashya (Difficulty / Challenge)
entries.notes: A difficulty or objection raised against a legal ruling; may remain unanswered (teku)

entries.name: Tiouvta / Tuvya (Decisive Refutation)
entries.notes: A conclusive refutation of an Amoraic position by a Tannaitic source; 'tiouvta d'X tiouvta'

entries.name: Tana Tuna (The Tanna Taught)
entries.notes: Formula citing a specific Tannaitic teaching; introduces a Baraita or Mishnah as evidence

entries.name: Raavad (Rabbi Avraham ben David of Posquières)
entries.notes: Provence, 1125–1198; Wrote critical glosses (hassagot) on the Rambam's Mishneh Torah; independent halakhic authority

entries.name: Rid (Rabbi Yeshaya di Trani the Elder)
entries.notes: Italy, c. 1180–1260; Wrote Tosafot Rid and Piskei Rid; major Italian Rishon

entries.name: Rashbam (Rabbi Shmuel ben Meir)
entries.notes: France, 1085–1158; Grandson of Rashi; completed Rashi's commentary on Bava Batra; independent Talmud commentator

entries.name: Rabbeinu Chananel
entries.notes: North Africa, 990–1055; Wrote early Talmud commentary; bridged Geonic and Rishonic eras

entries.name: Ramah (Rabbi Meir Abulafia)
entries.notes: Spain, 1170–1244; Wrote Yad Ramah on Sanhedrin and other tractates; important Talmudic commentary

entries.name: Maharam of Lublin (Rabbi Meir ben Gedaliah)
entries.notes: Poland, 1558–1616; Wrote Meir Einei Chachamim on the Talmud; important early Acharon

entries.name: Penei Yehoshua (Rabbi Yaakov Yehoshua Falk)
entries.notes: Poland/Germany, 1680–1756; Major novellae on the Talmud; printed in standard editions

entries.name: Ketzot haChoshen (Rabbi Aryeh Leib haKohen Heller)
entries.notes: Galicia, 1745–1813; Deep analytical commentary on Choshen Mishpat; revolutionized study of civil law

entries.name: Nesivos haMishpat (Rabbi Yaakov Lorberbaum)
entries.notes: Poland, 1760–1832; Commentary on Choshen Mishpat; companion work to Ketzot haChoshen
